title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Fishing guides polish image KENAI RIVER: The pros are now required to take a course. By BRANDON LOOMIS Anchorage Daily News Published: December 11, 2006 Last Modified: December 11, 2006 at 01:59 AM SOLDOTNA -- Kenai River guides know they are viewed through a fisheye lens, up close and sometimes distorted, with no blemish undetected by local anglers who resent the professionals. Whether the issue is crowding, motor pollution, fishing ethics or fish allocations, the hundreds who work the river with clients say they know they're being watched closely. In an effort both to improve their image and to shore up their professional credentials, they're now required to go to school. Fourteen guides last week went through five days of training in everything from stream ecology to business practices …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Snowfall covers trails for a good city ski weekend ALPINE: Downhill slopes also get a blanket that allows runs to open. Daily News staff Published: December 2, 2006 Last Modified: December 2, 2006 at 01:55 AM Alpine and nordic skiers rejoice. Skiing conditions across the Anchorage area look good after Friday's snowfall. Sunshine and temperatures peaking in the low 20s are expected today. Alyeska Resort reported snowfall of five inches at the base and eight inches at the summit in anticipation of opening today and Sunday from 10:30 a.m. to 5:30 p.m. Hilltop Ski Area received five inches of snow and all its main runs are open. Hilltop is open from 9 a.m. to 9 p.m. today and Sunday. Snow deposited by Friday's storm varied widely throughout Southcentral, according to the National Weather Servi…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Snowshoes still a better choice than boots for hikes Published: May 7, 2006 Last Modified: May 7, 2006 at 03:43 AM For an accurate description of spring hiking opportunities in Southcentral Alaska this year, turn to the cross-country skiing reports: • Good crust skiing in the South Fork of Eagle River. • Still winter snows at the back of Center Ridge at Turnagain Pass. • A mix of crust and windblown snow in Powerline Pass above Anchorage. Suffice it to say that for everywhere but the snow-short Cooper Landing area on the Kenai Peninsula, any sort of hiking -- other than postholing through snow -- is pretty much out from an altitude of 1,000 feet up, with a few notable exceptions. The most obvious involve south-facing, windswept ridges such as Bird Ridge along the Seward Highway south of An…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Harrison takes Chugach post CHIEF: Coast Guard commander hired as state park superintendent. By PETER PORCO Anchorage Daily News Published: April 13, 2006 Last Modified: April 13, 2006 at 02:29 AM Click to enlarge Harrison The Alaska Division of Parks and Outdoor Recreation this week named a new superintendent of Chugach State Park, a 48-year-old U.S. Coast Guard officer with expertise in marine safety, security and natural-resource protection. Cmdr. Tom Harrison, an Eagle River resident who is the second in command at the Coast Guard's Marine Safety Office in Anchorage, will retire from the service and take over operations of the big park east of the city on May 1, the division said. Chugach has been without a superintendent since August when the person in that post, Jerry Lewanski, becam…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Get going Published: February 17, 2006 Last Modified: February 19, 2006 at 06:21 AM HIKING Denali National Park Park rangers lead hikes at 1 p.m. Saturdays and Sundays throughout the winter, Murie Science and Learning Center, Mile 1.3 Park Road. Entrance fee is $10, $20 families. (1-907-683-9532, www.nps.gov/dena ) Social hiking Alaska Outdoors hosts weekly family-oriented outings on local multiuse trails, 6:30-8 p.m. Mondays and Thursdays. Free. (770-0440) • Monday, University Lake Park. • Thursday, Prospect Heights. RUNNING/WALKING Volking around The Anchorage Volkssports Club hosts a variety of 10-11-kilometer walks to keep you in shape. The events are free, or you can pay $3 for credit and distance awards.
